title: CV
text:
Education

title: CV
text:
Research Experience
Research Assistant — Department of Economics, Sciences Po (Apr 2025 – Sept 2025)

Designed and executed causal-inference analyses using staggered Difference-in-Differences and event-study models in R to estimate the impact of forest-protection policies on climate-relevant outcomes.
Constructed a structured, subnational policy database for Africa from systematic literature reviews, government documents, and NGO publications.
Managed and processed large-scale geospatial datasets (WDPA, satellite-derived tree-cover loss).
Performed geospatial mapping and georeferencing of policy locations across African countries.

Research & Teaching Assistant — Department of Economics, KNUST (Nov 2023 – Aug 2024)

Coordinated household-survey fieldwork for a willingness-to-pay study (enumerator supervision, respondent tracking, quality checks).
Cleaned and merged primary survey data with the Ghana Living Standards Survey (GLSS) for microeconometric analysis in Stata.
Collected firm-level data on commercial water use and combined field surveys with municipal administrative records.
Led tutorial sessions in Mathematics for Economists and supported assessment through grading and feedback.
Conducted systematic literature reviews and coordinated weekly departmental research seminars.

Research Assistant — Kumasi Hive, Kumasi (Oct 2022 – Dec 2022)

Developed and fielded research questionnaires producing a clean dataset of 100 observations.
Performed data cleaning and quality checks for econometric analysis.
Built a reporting framework to track key performance indicators.

Taught Senior High Level Economics; designed customised lesson plans; monitored progress through regular assessments.

title: Research
text:
Sciences Po, Paris — Supervisor: Prof. Clément Imbert
I study how improvements in domestic market access affect non-cocoa, non-mining forest loss in Ghana between 2001 and 2021. The empirical strategy combines a structural trade model that links district-level prices to a market-access index with a panel instrumental-variable approach using a recentered instrument that exploits plausibly exogenous variation in transport-cost shocks.
Methods: structural trade modelling · panel IV with recentered instruments · Google Earth Engine · high-resolution land-cover data.
Slides · Code

title: Abdul Baari Bakpa
text:
I am a graduate student in the MRes in Economics at Sciences Po, Paris, where I am supervised by Prof. Clément Imbert. I previously completed a BA in Economics at Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST) in Ghana.
My research sits at the intersection of development, environmental, and trade economics, with an empirical focus on Sub-Saharan Africa. I am particularly interested in how infrastructure, market integration, and policy shape land use, deforestation, and structural transformation.
My ongoing master’s thesis, The Price of Connection: Market Access and Forest Loss in Ghana, combines a structural trade model with high-resolution satellite data and a panel instrumental-variable strategy to estimate the causal effect of market access on non-cocoa, non-mining forest loss.
